panels.title: Avg CPU
panels.description: Shows the average CPU utilization across all hosts. Look for sustained values above 80% that may indicate capacity pressure.

panels.title: Avg Memory
panels.description: Shows the average memory utilization across all hosts. Watch for values approaching 90% which may lead to OOM conditions.

panels.title: Avg Network
panels.description: Shows the average network bandwidth utilization across all hosts. Spikes may correlate with increased traffic or data-intensive workloads.

panels.title: Avg Conn Pool
panels.description: Shows the average connection pool utilization across database connections. High values indicate potential connection exhaustion.

panels.title: Load Average (1m / 5m / 15m)
panels.description: Shows the 1-minute, 5-minute, and 15-minute host load averages. Values exceeding the CPU core count suggest saturation.

panels.title: CPU Usage Percentage
panels.description: Tracks host-level CPU usage over time broken down by pod. Look for upward trends or periodic spikes that align with workload patterns.

panels.title: Memory Usage Percentage
panels.description: Tracks host-level memory usage over time broken down by pod. A steady upward climb may indicate a memory leak.

panels.title: CPU Time by State
panels.description: Shows CPU time split across states such as user, system, and iowait. High iowait signals disk or network bottlenecks.

panels.title: Memory Usage by State
panels.description: Shows host memory broken down by state such as used, cached, and buffered. Watch for shrinking free memory over time.

panels.title: Swap Usage by State
panels.description: Tracks swap memory usage over time. Any significant swap activity indicates memory pressure on the host.

panels.title: Disk I/O (Read / Write)
panels.description: Tracks disk read and write throughput in bytes over time. Sudden spikes may indicate heavy logging or database flushes.

panels.title: Disk Operations (Read / Write)
panels.description: Tracks the number of disk read and write IOPS over time. High operation counts with low throughput suggest small random I/O.

panels.title: Disk I/O Time
panels.description: Shows the cumulative time the disk spent on I/O operations. Rising values indicate increasing disk latency on the host.

panels.title: Filesystem Usage by Mountpoint
panels.description: Shows disk space consumed per filesystem mountpoint. Watch for mountpoints approaching capacity to prevent write failures.

panels.title: Filesystem Utilization
panels.description: Tracks the percentage of disk capacity in use across hosts. Values above 85% warrant immediate attention.

panels.title: Network I/O (Transmit / Receive)
panels.description: Shows network throughput split by transmit and receive directions. Look for asymmetric patterns that may indicate unusual traffic flows.

panels.title: Network Packets (Transmit / Receive)
panels.description: Tracks the packet rate for transmitted and received traffic. High packet rates with low throughput suggest many small requests.

panels.title: Network Errors by State
panels.description: Shows network-level errors categorized by state. Any non-zero values warrant investigation into host NIC or network issues.

panels.title: Dropped Packets
panels.description: Tracks the number of packets dropped at the host network interface. Dropped packets often indicate buffer exhaustion or rate limiting.

panels.title: Process Count by Status
panels.description: Shows the number of host processes grouped by status such as running, sleeping, or blocked. A rising blocked count signals resource contention.

panels.title: Network Connections by State
panels.description: Tracks TCP connections by state such as established, time_wait, and close_wait. A buildup of time_wait connections may indicate connection churn.

panels.title: Resource Usage by Service
panels.description: Breaks down CPU, memory, and network consumption per service. Identify which services are the heaviest resource consumers.

panels.title: Resource Usage by Instance
panels.description: Breaks down CPU, memory, and network consumption per host instance. Use this to spot hot instances that need rebalancing.
